data.title: NICE Faces A Waiting Game For Real AI-Driven Gains
data.description:
Shares traded at $133.70 when I last called NICE Ltd. (NICE) a Hold. They’re now at $116.57 and at one point dipped to $99, the lowest in a year. That’s a steep drop, but even after this slide, there’s no slam-dunk bargain here. The price already builds in decent growth for cloud and AI (about mid-teens annually), just not a wild breakout. The company’s numbers are good, but I still haven’t seen hard proof that the big AI push or the Cognigy deal is making a major difference in profits or growth. Until those show up, the stock looks stuck in the middle. It’s not cheap enough to get excited about and not risky enough to dump.

Recurring Revenue and AI Drive the Business, But Payoff Remains Elusive

NICE sells cloud software for customer service, automation, financial crime, and public safety, and nearly 90% of its revenue is recurring. Gross margins are strong (over 67%), and cash flow is solid. Buying Cognigy for $955 million was a big move. This gives NICE what they say is the only fully integrated, purpose-built AI stack for customer experience. The idea is simple: put Cognigy’s AI together with NICE’s own CXone platform, sell more to existing clients, and win new deals by being the one-stop shop for big, AI-powered customer service projects. Management says every big new deal now has AI in it, and sales of Autopilot and Copilot products have tripled. The thing is, those wins haven’t yet shown up as a surge in company-wide revenue or profits.

This market is growing fast. More companies want better, smarter customer service and automation, with AI running the show. NICE has 27,000 clients, including most of the Fortune 100 and keeps getting top marks from Gartner and IDC for its contact center and AI offerings. Competitors are everywhere: big, established firms and upstart cloud companies. So every win is hard-fought. The main thing that sets NICE apart is its big data sets from years of customer interactions and a native AI platform. Scott Russell, the CEO, says, “The other contact center as a service players do not have an AI-native platform of their own…We do. The advantage that, that brings us to win in this market is immense.” Even so, customer acquisition costs are up, switching to cloud is still happening in fits and starts, and old and new rivals keep the pressure on prices and innovation.

Steady Growth and Strong Margins Define Financial Performance

The latest numbers tell a story of steady, not explosive, growth. Third quarter revenue hit $732 million, up 6% from last year. Cloud revenue was up 13%, and annual recurring revenue from AI jumped 49% (or 43% if you don’t count Cognigy). Non-GAAP EPS came in at $3.18, up 10%, and operating cash flow climbed 20% to $190 million. Margins are still best in class for the sector, but haven’t moved much. That says the big bets on AI and cloud haven’t yet created a step change in profitability. Full-year guidance nudged up, but the long-term growth forecast didn’t change.

At $116.57 a share, NICE trades at 13.3 times trailing earnings, 8.4 times EBITDA and about 2.4 times forward sales. Wall Street expects 2026 EPS of $11.12 (that’s actually a bit down from 2025’s guidance of $12.33 to $12.53) and revenue of $3.18 billion, up 7 to 8%. If the company can keep margins in the low 30s and put up mid-teens earnings growth for a few years, the stock could move to $135 to $150. Some upside, but nothing wild. If AI and Cognigy really kick in and growth speeds up, $160-plus is possible. But if the non-AI parts of the business slip, $100 is back in play. Right now, the price reflects expectations that things will keep getting a little better. Not blowout performance, but no disaster either.

Investor Mood Remains Split

There are headlines about NICE being at “high risk of performing badly,” but also plenty of press about earnings beats, guidance raises and new partnerships. Analysts can’t agree: some say NICE is ahead in AI, others think earnings estimates are still too optimistic. When NICE reported last quarter, the stock went up briefly, then dropped back down. That’s another sign that nobody’s convinced things are about to change.

What could go wrong? The big one is the Cognigy deal not delivering, whether because the two companies don’t mesh or the product roadmap slips. If big enterprise clients don’t move to the cloud and AI fast enough, revenue could stall. Rivals are aggressive on pricing and features. Debt is manageable, but higher than before. New rules around data or AI could also get in the way. If AI bookings slow down or margins shrink by 2 or 3 percentage points, earnings could drop by $2 to $3 a share, and the stock would probably slide under $100.

Path Forward Depends on AI Uptake and Margins

The plan is for $3.18 billion in sales next year, up 7 to 8%, but flat or even a little lower earnings due to costs from integrating Cognigy. The company expects double-digit earnings growth to come back in 2027, once the dust settles. What I’ll be watching: whether AI-led sales keep growing 15 to 20%, whether existing clients buy more Cognigy products, and whether margins start to widen again in late 2026. Big wins in enterprise AI could move the needle. More likely, though, progress will be slow and steady, not sudden.

If AI and cloud growth disappoint, with margins dropping to 27 or 28%, the stock could fall under $100. The base case (8% revenue growth, 31 to 32% margins, $11 to $12 EPS) supports a fair value of $120 to $135. The bull case, with faster adoption and $13-plus in EPS by 2027, could mean $150 to $160 or more if investors reward that growth. Each outcome depends on the economy, churn, how well new products sell, and management hitting their targets.

NICE runs a solid business with lots of loyal customers and a strong spot in cloud and AI for customer service. But after the recent slide, the numbers and the story don’t add up to a great buying opportunity. Upside is there if Cognigy and AI deliver more than expected and margins expand, but until that’s clear in the results, the risk and reward are balanced. If proof shows up or the stock gets closer to $100 for no good reason, I’d reconsider. If things go wrong with Cognigy or core growth fades, I’d get more cautious. For now, I’m staying put.

data.title: Equinor: A Strategic European Energy Buy With Margin Of Safety
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Equinor ASA (EQNR), I highlighted their solid financial health and strong exposure to Norway as its key oil and gas producer, but decided to go for other options instead.

As the stock is down a bit and has underperformed the S&P by over 10% since then, I believe the “need” for an asset like this is rising significantly, offering protection from macro pressure while offering a pillar for Europe’s oil and gas industry, playing a vital role in its future almost no matter what, which is why I believe a Buy rating is justified.

Internal Developments

Equinor continued developing well into Q3’25, with NCS production up 9%, US onshore production up 40%, and 9% for offshore, while the rest of their international production fell due to their completed/future asset divestments, while power generation also increased, and they expect their overall 2025 oil and gas production to grow by 4% in 2025.

As they show, the current capital distributions are not very sustainable if we exclude the working capital changes, while the company also has to pay massive amounts of taxes due to the strong years they had recently. Equinor is still expecting the total capital distribution for the year to be around $9 billion, for a combined yield of roughly 14.75% based on their ~$61 billion market cap at the moment of writing this.

Once again, most of their buybacks were in Q3, with the State’s buybacks playing a key role here, as Equinor is actively buying back shares from Norway as a way of paying it more than just dividends and taxes, as they have an agreement to repurchase a proportionate amount of shares from Norway depending on their open market buybacks, aiming at maintaining the state’s ownership unchanged. As for the dividend, they are currently trading at a yield of about 7.4%, which is definitely not bad given the environment.

The CFO also mentioned that they have a multi-year approach to dividends and buybacks due to taxes and want to use them as a natural part of their distribution framework, although I still believe a cut is possible, especially when it comes to the buybacks if the environment continues to deteriorate. The quote itself is 4 paragraphs long, but I’ll leave you with the first one and encourage you to check out the rest in their Q3 Earnings Call:

Secondly, capital distribution will have a priority in our capital allocation model. Cash dividend -- the cash dividend, you should consider that as a bankable. I mean, that will come. On top of that, we will use share buyback and share buyback will be used on a regular basis. It is a natural part of our capital distribution framework as such. So -- and we clearly aim to be competitive when it comes to the overall capital distribution.

The free cash flow (the one above is their net cash flow) got to $8.02 billion in 9M'25 (compared to $8.91 billion over 9M'24) due mostly to higher CAPEX, with Equinor expecting $13 billion in organic CAPEX in 2025.

As the company’s CEO recently mentioned that Equinor plans to drill 250 oil and gas exploration wells in Norway’s waters by 2035 in an effort to maintain production at 2020 levels, we can likely expect high CAPEX to persist for longer, as the company has to replace their depleted resources, calling it “one of the largest industrial plans Norway has ever seen.”

Financially, based on EQNR’s latest report, we continue to see a solid position, with the current assets still covering their current liabilities quite well, while we also see over $10.7 billion worth of assets and ~$1.5 billion in liabilities that are held for sale, as the company is actively rebalancing its portfolio, as mentioned earlier.

Macro Developments

Probably the main headline these days has been the situation in Venezuela, the country with the largest proven reserves of oil (many times more than the US), although the quality is much weaker and the production is only a few percentages of what the US produces.

Although it’s still in what I would call its early stages of development, this factor has the potential to weaken oil prices even more and result in lower prices that can hurt oil and gas producers, as that would then limit their capital to develop projects or new wells.

However, I’ll mention again that even if the US manages to replace the country’s leadership quickly, it can still take years for US oil companies to actually invest in the country and produce anything significant, as they still have to justify doing so not only financially (significant capital is needed for refining/infrastructure), but also convince their shareholders that the high political and economic risk is worth taking.

We’ve seen White House spokeswoman Taylor Rogers say that “all of our oil companies are ready and willing to make big investments in Venezuela that will rebuild their oil infrastructure,” but I still believe that experts are right when they say that these companies won’t rush into the country and invest billions overnight.

Right now, oil is at a point where companies are already rethinking their capital spending plans and rather going into self-preservation mode instead of aggressively expanding, so oil from Venezuela flooding the market is one of the last things these companies would want, with companies like Exxon (XOM) even calling Venezuela currently “uninvestable” in a quick way to summarize their stance.

Still, a day after the Venezuela operation, OPEC reaffirmed their plan to hold production steady in Q1 2026, which could serve as a positive catalyst at least in the near term, as their hikes were a big factor in oil’s crash in 2025.

In case you missed it, even before this situation, J.P. Morgan warned that oil could go into the $30s by late 2027 in a more extreme scenario where we would see significant oversupply. More stable scenarios still hint at average WTI prices of around $53/bbl to $54/bbl in 2026 and 2027, which is not terrible for most oil companies, but it can still result in weaker budgets for investing, dividends or buybacks.

Again, there have been several studies highlighting the continued pressure on the US consumer due to rising costs of living, lower income, etc. that were quite significant even before tariffs, while we now see rising uncertainty around the Fed’s independence after a recent criminal investigation into Powell from the DOJ, a couple of weeks after Bank of America’s CEO warned that the market will “punish people” if the country doesn’t have an independent Fed once Powell’s term ends in May 2026.

However, this tense political environment can actually be an opportunity for Equinor, as we can see the market’s interest move away from the US due to the new political/economic risks intensifying while the consumer environment remains weak.

And this is where Equinor could turn out to be a very important strategic asset to own (alongside Norway as their majority shareholder, as mentioned in the previous analysis). It’s not necessarily the best oil company out there, but they offer a healthy and undervalued way to gain exposure to one of the pillars of Europe’s oil and gas industry going forward, which can be even more valuable now with all the macro tensions.

Regarding that, Equinor said it could be forced to terminate the Empire Wind project if Trump's recent order to halt construction stands, estimating an impact of about $5.3 billion and calling it an existential threat for the project.

As the project was already approved and they estimated that it was about 60% complete, Equinor filed for injunction against the order. As a note, since we’re talking about a company that’s majority-owned by Norway, this can further affect the relationship between the two states, supporting the previous argument.

As a note for the longer-term, we’ve seen TotalEnergies (TTE) - another solid European oil and gas company that I covered before - release a very interesting outlook where they expect oil demand to peak a whole decade later than previously estimated. This is supposed to be backed by the shift in policies across the globe, as well as falling EV demand trends, Trump’s LNG/permitting focus, and similar initiatives, supporting higher demand for longer.

We also had some talks about a potential peace in Ukraine later in 2025, but that didn’t advance much recently, while the market’s attention moved to Venezuela and later the situation in Iran. Although Russian oil is sanctioned now and will likely stay like that for a while even after the war, this could eventually lead to more oil and gas flooding the international market, potentially putting even more pressure on the prices.

Risks Reiterated

Although Equinor could stand to benefit as one of Europe’s pillars when it comes to oil and gas, they will ultimately depend a lot on global markets and politics, with potential risks for falling prices from several directions.

Meanwhile, taxes and potential for more political pressure from outside the country can lead to more issues for EQNR, eventually being forced to lower their dividend once again - as it’s ultimately normal for a cyclical business - potentially leading to less market confidence.

Valuation

Annualizing EQNR’s 9M’25 net cash flow before distributions, I’ll begin with roughly $7.12 billion in net cash flow in 2025, below their ~$7.66 billion average FCF expected based on higher commodity prices, assuming that they manage to increase this by a CAGR of 3% over the next decade, basically keeping production intact and gaining from inflation or even buybacks if we’re talking on a per share basis, as well as potential debt reductions or other investments to counter their higher CAPEX since 2025’s production should be almost identical to 2024’s, with a 2% terminal growth rate, a solid 11% discount rate and 2.53 billion shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $86.48 billion, for an intrinsic value of $34.18 per share, which is well above the current $25.30 at the moment of writing this, signaling a solid margin of safety if Equinor manages to deliver these small growth expectations.

Conclusions

Equinor is a Buy, backed by the increasing strategic importance thanks to macro tensions, offering a strong yield to reward shareholders, while production should remain stable and predictable over the next decade. There are risks, and CAPEX can certainly get out of hand if they are forced to invest “too much” to maintain their production, but the overall risk-reward is more and more in favor of the investment given the current environment.

data.title: DIVB: Outperformance, Despite Lower Yields
data.description:
With an investment strategy that blends dividend yields and buybacks, the iShares Core Dividend ETF (DIVB)’s portfolio maintains a still conservative profile, but without such a strong emphasis on defensive areas of the market. This approach has worked over time, outperforming pure dividend plays, while offering an alternative for investors seeking total returns rather than only income. The next sections will break down DIVB’s holdings and compare its valuation, performance, volatility, and tradability with the broader market and dividend peers.

ETF Description & Highlights

DIVB provides exposure to companies with a strong track record of dividend distributions and share buybacks, following the Morningstar US Dividend and Buyback Index.

Starting from an eligible universe of companies that have recently paid dividends and offered share buybacks totaling more than 0.1%, the index measures the total shareholder yield for each company, considering a 75% weight for the TTM dividend yield and a 25% weight for the 2-year net buyback yield. After filtering out companies ranked within the top 5% of the pool, the remaining companies are selected for inclusion in the index from top to bottom until they reach a total of 50% of the total shareholder payout dollar of the broad market, as measured by the sum of total shareholder yield multiplied by the market capitalization for each company.

As a final step, companies that constitute the index are weighted according to their total shareholder payout dollar, subject to caps that limit any individual stock to a maximum weight of 4.9% and maintain each sector size within 5% of its participation in the broad market universe.

As of January 2, 2026, DIVB had $1.3 billion in assets under management, spread across 406 equity holdings, with an average market capitalization of $107.6 billion. The portfolio shows a clear tilt toward large-cap stocks, which make up 62.6% of total assets. This is followed by mid caps at 25.9%, while small caps account for a more modest 6.8% and mega caps only 4.4%. For comparison, the Russell 1000, a benchmark for large and mid caps, represented by the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), has an average market cap three times larger, due to a much heavier concentration in mega caps, which make up 41.8% of the index.

DIVB’s top ten holdings are Cisco (4.68%), Exxon Mobil (4.00%), Qualcomm (3.65%), JPMorgan (3.47%), Accenture (2.50%), IBM (2.39%), Wells Fargo (2.14%), Texas Instruments (2.06%), Bank of America (1.91%), and Verizon (1.85%). Together, these positions represent about 29% of total assets. Unlike market-cap indexes, like the Russell 1000, DIVB’s top holdings are less concentrated in big tech names and include areas like energy, not to mention other technology companies outside software and semiconductors.

The table below presents six dividend funds that will serve as the peer group for DIVB in this analysis. The first is the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM), the largest fund in the group, tracking a broad market cap-weighted index that screens for above-average dividend yields. The SPDR S&P Dividend ETF (SDY) takes a more selective approach, following the S&P High Yield Dividend Aristocrats Index, where constituents must demonstrate at least twenty consecutive years of dividend growth and are weighted by yield. Next, Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) focuses on quality dividend stocks, tracking the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index that favors companies with strong balance sheets and consistent cash flows, while the iShares Select Dividend ETF (DVY) follows an index that leans toward mid- and small-cap stocks. The iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV) focuses on high-yielding U.S. companies with financial health, resulting in a portfolio dominated by large-cap exposure. Finally, First Trust Morningstar Dividend Leaders Index ETF (FDL) tracks the Morningstar Dividend Leaders Index, weighting stocks by dividend dollar payments and concentrating primarily on companies with a long dividend track record.

DIVB’s largest allocation is to technology, which accounts for 27.6% of the portfolio. A second tier includes more defensive areas: financial services (18.4%), alongside consumer staples and healthcare, both at 9.6%. Moving further down the allocation size, industrials (8.2%) and energy (8.1%) add cyclical exposure and income-oriented cash flows. The fund also has smaller allocations to consumer discretionary (5.8%), communication services (5.5%), real estate (3.2%), utilities (2.6%), and basic materials (1.6%), completing a somewhat diversified sector mix.

When compared to the Russell 1000, DIVB overweights energy (+5.1%), consumer staples (+5.0%), and financial services (+4.9%), indicating a clear income bias. On the other hand, the fund is underweight in technology (-5.7%), communication services (-5.1%), and consumer discretionary (-4.9%). These gaps at nearly 5% are consistent with the fund’s rules of limiting sector exposure gaps relative to the broader equity indices at the -5% to +5 % range to avoid excessive sector concentration relative to the benchmark.

If we break down DIVB’s holdings across its largest sectors, we see that in technology, the fund does not show the same heavy concentration in semiconductors and software, as seen in the Russell 1000 and many ETFs. Instead, it has no exposure to software and an underweight allocation to semiconductors via positions like Qualcomm, Texas Instruments, and Analog Devices, though remaining the largest segment within the sector. Other core areas include networking equipment through Cisco Systems and IT services, with holdings in Accenture and IBM. Rounding out the sector exposure, the fund also owns names in the hardware field, such as Dell Technologies, HP, and HP Enterprise.

In the next large sector, financial services, the fund looks much more diversified, with 95 holdings. Still, banks are by far the largest positions, with stakes in global banks (JPMorgan, Wells Fargo, Bank of America, and Citigroup). Holdings in investment banks and brokerages include Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley. It also has a relatively small exposure to insurance, with positions in MetLife, Aflac, Prudential Financial, and Hartford, as well as positions in payments/data areas through Fidelity National Information Services and Global Payments.

Healthcare is another sector that is worth mentioning. The exposure is concentrated in its top positions, despite having 19 holdings. The largest segment is pharmaceuticals, led by Johnson & Johnson, AbbVie, Merck, and Pfizer. Managed care is represented by UnitedHealth Group, Cigna, and Elevance Health, while smaller allocations include biotech and medical device names, such as Amgen, Gilead Sciences, and Medtronic.

Outside the core sectors, DIVB’s exposure to industrials is led by holdings in aerospace & defense (RTX, Lockheed Martin, and Northrop Grumman), along with positions in UPS, FedEx, Caterpillar, and the conglomerate Honeywell. Complementing its top holdings, the fund also has significant individual positions in energy (Exxon Mobil and ConocoPhillips), and telecom services operators as well, through Verizon, AT&T, and T-Mobile US.

DIVB has a distinct large allocation to technology (27.6%) for a dividend fund, leading to a heavy overweight allocation of 19.8% relative to peers. On the other hand, the fund has a significant underweight position to typical income sectors, such as utilities (-7.3%), energy (-7.0%), consumer staples (-6.7%), and healthcare (-3.1%), due to the large exposure to peers in general to these sectors. Of note, we can see that the sector allocation strategy among dividend peers is far from uniform. For instance, SCHD, HDV, and FDL have at least 20% allocated in energy, while DVY has over 25% in utilities and financial services. As a result, overall, most peers have a moderate to low sector correlation to DIVB (below 0.40). The exception is VYM, which keeps a strong correlation of 0.85 to DIVB, reflecting a relatively large stance in technology (17.5%) and financial services (21.2%), nearly in line with DIVB.

Yields Below Dividend Peers

DIVB offers a dividend yield of 2.4%, one of the lowest dividend yields in the peer group. This is, of course, driven by a sector mix with a lighter exposure to above-average yield sectors, as a result of its investment objective that blends yields with share buybacks rather than most peers that focus on dividend yields. But it is important to note that DIVB has the highest dividend growth among peers, with double-digit growth rates in several areas, such as technology, industrials, and financial services. Despite this disadvantage in terms of dividend yields in absolute terms relative to peers, there is no doubt that its 2.4% yield remains more than twofold the benchmark, given an allocation to financial services, energy, and consumer staples that is roughly 5% higher than in the Russell 1000, as mentioned earlier.

Attractive Valuation

At 14.8x P/E, DIVB trades at about a 31% discount to the Russell 1000’s 21.6x. Part of that discount is tied to its lighter exposure to technology and consumer discretionary relative to the benchmark. Another contribution comes from a stock selection that favors total shareholder yields, resulting in lower P/E multiples across most sectors, especially technology, consumer discretionary, healthcare, and communication services. This is evidenced by top holdings such as Qualcomm (14.3x P/E), Verizon (8.6x), and Pfizer (8.1x).

DIVB’s growth profile is meaningfully behind the Russell 1000. Its historical earnings growth of 1.2% sits well below the Russell 1000’s 8.2%, and sales growth (4.1%) also trails the benchmark’s 7.4%. The sector mix is a key factor behind that, given its conservative allocation, underweighting technology and consumer discretionary, as highlighted in the valuation analysis. Further, the absence of high-growth names like NVIDIA, Amazon, and Microsoft is a drag as well, as these companies are key growth drivers for the Russell 1000 and other broader equity indexes.

The fund’s profitability indicators do not compare favorably with the benchmark as well. Its EBITDA margin (29.1%) is about 6.9 percentage points below the Russell 1000 (36.0%), and its ROE (24.6%) also lags the benchmark’s 42.7% by roughly 18.1 points. This is driven even more by holdings in technology than in the valuation and growth analysis. This includes a sector exposure below the benchmark and a portfolio that lacks high-margin big tech names, while featuring top holdings with below-average profitability measures, such as Exxon Mobil (EBITDA margin 18.9%, ROE 11.4%), Accenture (17.6% and 25.0%), Cisco Systems (27.2% and 22.4%), and UnitedHealth (6.7% and 17.5%).

DIVB’s valuation is in the middle of the pack relative to peers. The fund’s P/E sits below VYM (16.4x), SDY (17.2x), and especially HDV (17.5x), which has heavier exposure to large caps (78.8%). At the lower end of the valuation range, DVY (12.2x) and FDL (11.5x) trade at a discount to peers, reflecting a combination of low exposure to technology and heavier positions in mid and small caps than most peers. Overall, DIVB’s mid-range suggests a modest upside potential on aggregate, in light of the fund’s higher earnings and sales growth figures compared to the peer group average: historical earnings of 1.2% versus 0.0% and sales growth of 4.1% versus 3.7%, respectively.

Outperforming Peers

DIVB has not kept up with broader equity indexes like the Russell 1000 over the past years. Despite outperforming in years such as 2021 and 2022, its annualized returns of 15.0% (3-year) and 12.5% (5-year) fall short of the Russell 1000’s 21.8% and 13.6%, respectively. But the fund has done better against dividend peers. DIVB is the best performer over the 3-year window and the second-best over the 5-year period, thanks to strong outperformance in bullish years, like in the 2023-2025 timeframe.

The fund’s volatility has been consistent with its somewhat conservative profile, with beta in the 0.87-0.89 range, which is considerably lower than the benchmark. Still, dividend peers have seen more contained price moves, with betas below 0.80, helped by their defensive sector mix, with limited exposure to volatile sectors like technology and consumer discretionary, and overweight allocation to defensive areas such as consumer staples, utilities, and healthcare. DIVB’s outperformance during bullish environments is illustrated by its upside capture ratio that has been in the 82-92 range, well above peers. The flip side is a downside capture ratio (112-94 range) that has been at the upper end of the peer range, which helps explain why the fund underperformed peers in 2022, when the broader market declined.

With an average daily trading volume of roughly $8.3 million, DIVB is a liquid fund, though not anywhere near major peers such as SCHD and VYM, which trade hundreds of millions of dollars a day. This helps explain the fund’s bid-ask spread of about 0.10%, wider than all peers. The fund’s 36% turnover sits close to the peer-group average, indicating moderate trading activity and rebalancing, while peers like VYM, SDY, and DVY keep it under 30%. Its expense ratio of 0.05% is fairly low, making the fund particularly cheap compared to SDY (0.35%) and DVY (0.38%), but in line with VYM, SCHD, and HDV. At the same time, tracking error is consistently lower than most peers, while its Sharpe ratios of 0.82 (3-year) and 0.65 (5-year) are the highest in the peer group, thanks to a combination of its outperformance and a slightly above-average volatility.

Final Thoughts

DIVB’s focus on total shareholder yield instead of purely on headline dividend yield results in a portfolio that ends up less anchored to traditional high-yield sectors. Rather, the fund shows a middle-ground sector mix, with a more conservative profile than the Russell 1000, but far from a proxy of defensive sectors like staples, utilities, and healthcare, as we can usually see to a greater extent in dividend funds. Still, valuation is in line with peers, representing a large discount to the broader market. This somewhat balanced approach has helped DIVB to outperform the peer group over the past years and may remain an advantage compared to peers in the coming quarters. After all, the overall environment is still constructive for the equity market, despite potential headwinds related to geopolitical tensions, along with uncertainties about the Fed's direction going forward.

In this context, DIVB should benefit from a market that continues to see earnings growth, but may become more selective, as valuations look stretched. Assuming S&P 500 earnings growth of 12%-15% in 2026, we can project DIVB’s earnings at mid-single digits over the same period, consistent with a historical 7% gap relative to the index. Thus, considering dividend yields of 2.4%, it is reasonable to forecast a high-single digit total return for the fund this year, absent any valuation multiple compression, given the fund’s low P/E multiple. The flip side is that the fund is not expected to keep pace with broader equity indexes, especially if the market leadership remains concentrated in big techs, as has been the case for much of the past years. In sum, the combination of its potential upside, low valuation, and moderate volatility keeps DIVB among the good alternatives for investors seeking some degree of defensiveness and yield without giving up total returns. However, for those aiming to maximize income, other peers covered in this article, most yielding above 3%, appear to be a better option at this moment.

data.title: VRIG: Remains A Solid Hold
data.description:
Thesis

We last covered the Invesco Variable Rate Investment Grade ETF (VRIG) a year ago, when we downgraded the name to 'Hold' on the back of a disappointing performance during the April 2025 market turmoil. VRIG is a short-term parking vehicle that has historically delivered very robust results with minimal volatility. In today's article we are going to revisit the name and articulate why we still find the name a solid 'Hold', even in today's macro environment marked by lower Fed Funds.

Current fund composition

Since our last coverage piece, the ETF has increased its securitized sleeve and decreased its treasury holdings:

Securitized simply means ABS securities (i.e. asset-backed securities), usually senior tranches. ABS bonds offer a spread pick-up over treasuries due to their complex risk features and structuring methodology.

A year ago, when we wrote our last article, the treasuries sleeve was at 26%, while the securitized sleeve was at 32%. The fund has therefore turned a bit more aggressive by adding securities offering a spread over treasuries. Please remember VRIG is an active fund; thus, exposures and segment allocations can change every month as portfolio managers see fit.

The fund, however, still keeps a highly rated portfolio:

Roughly 80% of the portfolio is rated A or AA, meaning the ETF holds high quality assets. We like the fact that BBB names represent a very small piece of the portfolio here.

The fund keeps a very low duration profile of only 0.2 years, but this represents the floating rate duration profile. Other analytics for the name are;

  • AUM: $1.4 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.3%
  • 30 day SEC yield: 4.8%
  • Duration: 0.2 years
  • Nr of securities: 352 holdings

The fund continues to provide for roughly 100 bps in excess spread over Fed funds and does this via a granular and diversified portfolio. However, as we saw in April 2025, the drawdowns here can be in excess of -1%. Readers need to remember that unlike treasuries, funds like VRIG do have credit spread sensitivity, although they revert back to par fairly quickly, usually in-line with duration metrics. For example, the portfolio's weighted average maturity is 0.6 years, which means the portfolio turns every 7.2 months. That simply means that even deep drawdowns will be resolved back to par within 7.2 months.

Lower Fed Funds means lower yields

An environment where the Fed cuts means lower all-in yields for all the funds covering the short end of the yield curve. We are in a macro environment where rates are forecasted to come down significantly. The Fed is set to cut aggressively in 2026.

The futures market currently gives us the following picture in terms of SOFR outlook:

The market is currently expecting a neutral rate close to 3%, which means another 70 bps lower in Fed Funds. One can extrapolate to a forward yield for the ETF of 4.1%, with an implied lag to Fed Funds.

One should not panic here because this is what VRIG is designed to do - offer an excess spread to the front end of the curve. So from that angle, one should only be concerned with the spread offered by the fund and the amount of risk it takes to deliver that spread.

The ETF offers a positive relative value

Now we have taken a look at the fund composition, its analytics, and its forward in terms of all-in yields. Let us frame that via a comparison with the fund's competitors:

We have chosen for comparison purposes a few funds we cover on the Seeking Alpha platform, namely the State Street SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL), the T. Rowe Price Ultra Short-Term Bond ETF (TBUX), and the PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active Fund (MINT). As we can see from the chart above, VRIG does outperform on the chosen time-frame.

This simply means VRIG is a solid hold that offers an attractive risk-reward profile when it comes to portfolio construction. Interesting to note that TBUX comes second when net performance numbers are considered. The lowest total return is provided by a T-Bills fund, as expected. However, via T-Bills an investor does not get any drawdowns.

What does a recession mean for VRIG?

Most investors are fearful of a potential recession. Such an event would have a temporary effect on VRIG. As discussed above, a recession would most likely cause a -1% to -2% drawdown for the fund, drawdown that would automatically correct itself in a few months, even during a recession. As we saw from the duration analysis, the portfolio is very short-dated, which means the underlying securities would mature fast, thus pulling to par.

Secondly, a recession would generate wider spreads for assets, thus allowing VRIG to buy into new securities at more attractive spread levels. Investors should therefore not think a recession can cause an impairment here. But one does have to stomach a drawdown in a recession with VRIG.

Conclusion

VRIG is a fixed income exchange-traded fund from Invesco. The name is overweight ABS securities, and provides for an excess spread over Fed Funds of roughly 100 bps. The ETF is actively managed; thus, portfolio managers can allocate as they see fit, all while maintaining an investment-grade profile. The ETF currently provides for a 4.8% 30-day SEC yield, but is set to move lower with Fed Funds. The name will not insulate investors from decreasing rates, and one has to be cognizant that they only get a spread over T-Bills here. Long-term the ETF has delivered, thus making it a solid 'Hold' for us.
